alignments.preprint_claim_text: LSD600 is the first corpus specifically designed for LSF-disease relation extraction, comprising 600 abstracts with 1900 relations of eight distinct types. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LSD600 is the first corpus specifically designed for LSF-disease relation extraction, comprising 600 abstracts with 1900 relations of eight distinct types. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same claim verbatim about the corpus size, relation count and novelty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The RoBERTa model trained on LSD600 achieved an F-score of 68.5% for the multi-label relation extraction task on the held-out test set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The RoBERTa model trained on LSD600 achieved an F-score of 68.5% for the multilabel relation extraction task on the held-out test set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The reported model and exact performance metric are the same in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The trained model on LSD600 achieved F-scores of 70.7% and 80.7% when validated on the Nutrition-Disease and Food-Disease datasets, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The trained model validated on the Nutrition-Disease and Food-Disease datasets achieved F-scores of 70.7% and 80.7%, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Validation results and dataset names are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LSD600 and the relation extraction system trained on it can serve as valuable resources to address the gap in understanding LSF-disease relations. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LSD600 and the relation extraction system trained on it can serve as valuable resources to address the gap in LSF-disease relation extraction. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about utility of the corpus and system is carried forward with equivalent scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Most studies on the modelling of Covid-19 were from Asia (52.70%) and Europe (25%). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Most studies on COVID-19 modeling were conducted in Asian (78.93%) and European (59.09%) countries. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both claims describe geographic distribution of studies but the reported percentages differ substantially, indicating the published paper updated/revised the values.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ratio predicted/observed values for cumulative cases was on average larger than 1, indicating cases of incorrect predictions and large uncertainty. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ratio of predicted over observed values for cumulative cases was on average larger than 1, indicating inaccurate and imprecise predictions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same finding about predicted/observed ratios >1 and its implication for inaccuracy/uncertainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ratio predicted/observed values was relatively smaller for SIR models than for SEIR models, indicating that more complex models might not be more accurate for predictions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report a comparison between SIR and SEIR models or a conclusion that more complex models are less accurate; this preprint-specific claim is absent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Values of both ratios decreased with the number of days covered by studies, indicating that the wider the time covered by the data, the higher the correctness and accuracy of predictions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a significant negative correlation between the ratio of predicted over observed cumulative deaths and the length of the period covered by the modeling. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both describe a negative relationship with study duration, but the publication specifies cumulative deaths (narrower focus) and reports a significant negative correlation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Bayesian model made predictions closer to reality than the compartmental and the statistical models, although these differences are only suggestive due to the small size of the data. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Bayesian model made predictions closer to reality than classical statistical models, although this difference is only suggestive due to the small number of predictions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The core claim that Bayesian models performed better is preserved, but the comparator wording changed and the publication presents the claim with higher certainty.

alignments.pub_claim_text: In 75% of predictions that provided confidence intervals, observed values fell within the 95% confidence interval of the predicted cumulative cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This result about the proportion of observed values within predicted CIs is a new quantitative claim present in the publication but not in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The initial set of 16 specimens showed a positive correlation between Raman and reflectance-derived fat content and triglyceride quantifications. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The initial set of 16 specimens showed a positive correlation between Raman and reflectance-derived fat content and triglyceride quantifications. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same finding for the initial specimen set.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Raman system effectively differentiated minimum-to-severe global and macrovesicular steatosis in the subsequent 66 specimens. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Raman system effectively differentiated minimum-to-severe global and macrovesicular steatosis in the subsequent 66 specimens. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Finding about differentiation performance on the 66 specimens is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A dual-variable prediction algorithm was developed, effectively classifying significant discrepancies between AI-estimated global HS and pathologist-estimated MaS. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A dual-variable prediction algorithm was developed, effectively classifying significant discrepancies between algorithm-estimated global HS and pathologist-estimated MaS. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication replaces 'AI-estimated' with 'algorithm-estimated'—a minor wording clarification while preserving the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study established the viability and reliability of a portable spectroscopic system for non-invasive HS and MaS assessment in human liver specimens. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study established the viability and reliability of a portable spectroscopic system for non-invasive HS and MaS assessment in human liver specimens. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion about the system's viability and reliability is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed method holds promise of refining donor liver assessment during liver recovery and ultimately elevating transplantation outcomes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method holds the promise of refining donor liver assessment during liver recovery and ultimately enhancing transplantation outcomes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the prospective benefit claim with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Patients with PNES report greater symptoms of somatisation compared to patients with ES. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients with PNES report greater symptoms of somatisation, general anxiety, and phobic anxiety compared to patients with ES. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents somatisation together with additional affected domains (general and phobic anxiety), broadening the reported symptom profile compared to the preprint's somatisation-only claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Elevated scores in the symptom domains of anxiety, phobic anxiety, and the general psychopathology factor p were found in PNES compared to ES groups. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients with PNES report greater symptoms of somatisation, general anxiety, and phobic anxiety compared to patients with ES. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: There is substantial overlap (general and phobic anxiety) though the publication emphasizes somatisation rather than the p factor; the core elevated-anxiety finding is retained but wording differs.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The somatisation domain produced the highest classification accuracy for distinguishing PNES from ES. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Somatisation produced the highest classification accuracy among the SCL-90-R domain scores for distinguishing PNES from ES. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates the same result that somatisation had the highest domain-level classification accuracy.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Self-reported psychopathology scores are not accurate enough for diagnosis in isolation but should raise suspicion of PNES in clinical settings. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Elevated psychopathology in the domains of somatisation, general anxiety, and phobic anxiety should raise the suspicion of PNES in clinical settings. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both emphasize that elevated self-reported psychopathology should prompt clinical suspicion; the publication omits the explicit caveat about diagnostic insufficiency but preserves the clinical implication.

alignments.pub_claim_text: A 6-item subset from the SCL-90-R produced comparable classification accuracy to the somatisation scores for identifying PNES. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This specific result about a 6-item subset is present in the publication but not in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Garbhini-GA1 formula, a non-linear function of crown-rump length (CRL), performs equivalently to published formulae for estimating first trimester gestational age. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Garbhini-GA1 formula, developed for the Indian population, performs equivalently to published formulae for estimating gestational age in the first trimester. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds that Garbhini-GA1 was developed for the Indian population but retains the equivalence claim for first-trimester GA estimation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CRL was identified as the most crucial parameter in estimating gestational age, with no other clinical or socioeconomic covariates contributing to the estimation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Crown-rump length (CRL) is identified as the most crucial parameter for estimating gestational age, with no other clinical or socioeconomic covariates contributing to the estimation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates CRL as the principal predictor with no additional covariates contributing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The estimated preterm birth rate across all formulae ranged from 11.27% to 16.50%, with Garbhini-GA1 estimating the lowest rate with the highest sensitivity and accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The estimated preterm birth rate using the Garbhini-GA1 formula is the lowest among all formulae tested, demonstrating the highest sensitivity and accuracy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both versions report Garbhini-GA1 yields the lowest preterm birth rate and highest sensitivity/accuracy; the publication omits the numeric range but preserves the comparative conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The last menstrual period (LMP)-based method overestimated gestational age by three days compared to the USG-Hadlock formula, and there was less than 50% agreement in the classification of preterm birth between these methods. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The last menstrual period (LMP)-based method overestimates gestational age by 3 days compared to the USG-Hadlock formula, with less than 50% agreement in the classification of preterm birth at the individual level. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the phrase 'at the individual level' but otherwise preserves the overestimation and low agreement claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study reinforces the need to develop population-specific gestational age formulae for better management of preterm birth. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study reinforces the need for developing population-specific gestational age formulae for better management of preterm birth. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The concluding recommendation for population-specific formulae is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Self-perceived loneliness was found to be the most important variable predicting time in lockdown. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Self-Perceived Loneliness was identified as the most influential variable for estimating the time spent in lockdown. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements claim the same result about self-perceived loneliness being the top predictor of time in lockdown with equivalent certainty and framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A significant U-shaped curve for the levels of perceived loneliness was observed, specifically decreasing during the 4th and 5th lockdown weeks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A significant U-shaped curve emerged for loneliness levels, with lower scores reported by participants who took part in the study during the 6th lockdown week. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both describe a U-shaped loneliness pattern but differ in the timing of the observed low point (4th–5th weeks vs. 6th week), indicating a revision of the temporal detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Lockdown measures may have affected how people evaluated their social support while in lockdown, leading to a decreased sense of loneliness. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings support the dissociation between social support and loneliness. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint posits that changes in perceived social support explain decreased loneliness, while the publication reports a dissociation between social support and loneliness, effectively reversing/altering the proposed relationship.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning approach and statistical validation on data from two different Western European countries ensure that the uncovered pattern is substantial. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Self-Perceived Loneliness varied across time in lockdown in the UK and Greek populations, with lower symptoms reported during the 4th and 6th lockdown weeks. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint emphasizes methodological validation across two countries to support the pattern; the publication presents the cross-country observation itself, shifting emphasis from methodological assurance to empirical observation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Measures and campaigns promoting better social support strategies could be potentially effective in keeping levels of perceived loneliness low, even in social isolation. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Social support strategies could be effective even in times of social isolation. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both claim social support strategies may remain effective during isolation, but the publication expresses lower certainty than the preprint, so the conclusion is retained but tempered in confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: All CPVT patients from Hong Kong presented at or before 19 years of age. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report this age-at-presentation result for the CPVT cohort; the claim is absent in the published version.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Clinical and electrocardiographic findings can be used to predict arrhythmic outcomes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models achieved more accurate VT/VF prediction. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint's general claim about clinical/ECG predictors is reframed in the publication to emphasize machine-learning models providing more accurate VT/VF prediction, amplifying the predictive claim with a methodological focus.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A nonparametric machine learning survival analysis achieved high accuracy for predicting the probability of incident VT/VF. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models achieved more accurate VT/VF prediction. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The specific ML survival analysis result is carried into the publication but presented more generally as ML models improving VT/VF prediction, which clarifies and slightly amplifies the predictive performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: No significant predictors were identified on Cox regression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: No significant predictors of VT/VF were identified in CPVT patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both state that no significant predictors were found for VT/VF in the CPVT cohort using the regression analysis; the content and certainty are preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Eight patients tested positive for the RyR2 gene, including a novel variant c.14861C>G. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report this specific genetic finding or the novel variant; the claim is absent in the final paper.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Arrhythmias other than VT/VF and Schwartz score are predictive of spontaneous ventricular tachycardia/ventricular fibrillation in congenital LQTS patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This publication claim pertains to congenital LQTS populations and does not appear in the CPVT-focused preprint, so it is an added claim in the published paper.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Clinical and ECG presentation vary between the pediatric/young and adult congenital LQTS population. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This LQTS-focused conclusion is not present in the preprint about CPVT and therefore represents an added publication claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A Random Forest classifier produced an AUROC of 0.863 for morbidity prediction and an AUROC of 0.812 for mortality prediction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The classifiers produced an AUROC of 0.863 for morbidity prediction and an AUROC of 0.812 for mortality prediction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numerical performance metrics and classifier outcomes are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Poverty rate, obesity rate, mean commute time to work, and proportion of people that wear masks significantly affected morbidity rates. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Poverty rate, obesity rate, mean commute time, and mask usage statistics significantly affected morbidity rates. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The same socio-economic and behavioral features are reported to influence morbidity in both versions with matching certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Ethnicity, median income, poverty rate, and education levels heavily influenced mortality rates. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Ethnicity, median income, poverty rate, and education levels heavily influenced mortality rates. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical claims about factors influencing mortality appear in both the preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Ethnicity, education, and economic disparity measures are major factors in predicting the COVID-19 mortality rate in a county. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Socio-economic features such as ethnicity, education, and economic disparity are the major factors for predicting county-level COVID-19 mortality rates. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about socio-economic features being major predictors of county-level mortality is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Differing correlations can be explained by the COVID-19 spread from metropolitan to less metropolitan areas. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The correlation between several socio-economic factors and COVID-19 morbidity and mortality gradually shifted and even reversed during the study period. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes temporal shifts and reversals in correlations, which clarifies and reframes the preprint's causal explanation about metropolitan spread as an observed temporal pattern.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Total parasite biomass is a better predictor of perturbations in host homeostasis in P. vivax patients than peripheral parasitaemia. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Total parasite biomass is a better predictor of perturbations in host homeostasis in P. vivax patients than peripheral parasitaemia. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The core conclusion that total parasite biomass predicts host perturbations better than peripheral parasitaemia is maintained with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Vivax high patients showed more profound alterations in haematological parameters, endothelial cell activation, and glycocalyx breakdown compared to Vivax low patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Vivax high patients showed more profound alterations in haematological parameters, endothelial cell activation, and glycocalyx breakdown compared to Vivax low patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The specific comparative results between Vivax high and low patients are preserved unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: High association of total parasite biomass with a subset of markers of endothelial cell activation, thrombocytopenia, and lymphopenia severity was observed. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: High association of total parasite biomass with a subset of markers of endothelial cell activation, thrombocytopenia, and lymphopenia severity was observed. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The reported associations between total parasite biomass and specific host markers are consistent between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models defined a combination of host parameters that could predict the extent of parasite infection outside of circulation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models defined a combination of host parameters measured in the circulation that could predict the extent of parasite infection outside of circulation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies that the predictive host parameters are measured in the circulation, adding specificity to the preprint's machine-learning-based predictive claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our results demonstrate how infected domains spread outward from epicenters/hotspots, leading to different regimes of sub-exponential growth. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The novel mathematical framework accounts for the in-homogeneous spatial spreading of COVID-19 in human populations. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The preprint result about outward spreading and sub-exponential growth is carried into the publication as a description of a framework that accounts for inhomogeneous spatial spread, reframing a specific result as part of a general modeling contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We show how weakly infected regions surrounding a densely populated area can cause rapid migration of the infection towards the center of the populated area. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model demonstrates that variable geographic density of the population can significantly enhance or suppress infection spreading. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both describe the impact of spatial heterogeneity on spread; the publication frames it generally in terms of geographic density enhancing or suppressing spread, clarifying the preprint's specific mechanism.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Localized strong quarantine conditions can prevent the spreading of the disease from an epicenter/hotspot, significantly reducing the number of infected people. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Localized lockdown/quarantine conditions can slow down the spreading of disease from epicenters. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the claim that localized quarantine/lockdown slows spread from epicenters, slightly reframing it as a model result rather than an absolute prevention statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Application of our model in different countries can provide a useful predictive tool for the authorities, particularly for planning strong lockdown measures in localized areas. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Predicted infection 'heat-maps' from the model show remarkable similarity to publicly available heat-maps from South Carolina. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint's broad claim about applicability in different countries is narrowed in the publication to a demonstrable application in South Carolina, providing a concrete validation rather than a broad multi-country application.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Experts anticipate that by 2029, AI/ML will lead to workplace changes including incursions into the disintermediation of physician expertise. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Experts anticipate that by 2029, AI/ML will lead to workplace changes including incursions into the disintermediation of physician expertise. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication claim text are identical in content and certainty, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: By 2029, AI/ML is expected to increase diagnostic accuracy, particularly for individuals with limited access to experts, minorities, and those with rare diseases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: By 2029, AI/ML is expected to increase diagnostic accuracy, particularly for individuals with limited access to experts, minorities, and those with rare diseases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim content and certainty are the same between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Expert panelists predict that AI/ML tools will improve access to expert doctor knowledge by 2029. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Expert panelists predict that AI/ML tools will improve access to expert doctor knowledge by 2029. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The assertion is presented identically in both versions with the same certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Preparation for the near-future of primary care will require improved levels of digital health literacy among patients and physicians. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Preparation for the near-future of primary care will require improved levels of digital health literacy among patients and physicians. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about required digital health literacy appears verbatim with unchanged certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A broad profile of alterations in the time-course of neural processing of faces is an early predictor of later Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) diagnosis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A broad profile of alterations in the time-course of neural processing of faces in infancy was predictive of later autism spectrum disorder (ASD). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same predictive result for face-processing alterations in infancy with equivalent certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Two brain response-defined subgroups in ASD show distinct alterations in different aspects of face processing compared to siblings without ASD diagnosis. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We identified two main subgroups in ASD, defined by distinct patterns of neural responses to faces, which differed on later sensory sensitivity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the two-subgroup finding but adds a specific downstream phenotype (sensory sensitivity) and reports higher certainty, making the claim more specific and stronger.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Moving from group-level comparisons to pattern recognition and stratification can help to understand and reduce heterogeneity in clinical cohorts. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Moving from group-level comparisons to pattern recognition and stratification can help to understand and reduce heterogeneity in clinical cohorts. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The methodological recommendation is identical but the reported certainty was reduced from the preprint to the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Neural processing of faces is diffusely atypical in Autism Spectrum Disorder and represents a strong candidate predictor of outcome at an individual level in the first year of life. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Individual differences between infants contribute to the diffuse pattern of alterations predictive of ASD in the first year of life. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the diffuse atypicality by emphasizing the role of individual differences in producing the predictive pattern, retaining the overall predictive claim and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AAC is present in more than 10.4% of the cohort despite low prevalence of diabetes and CKD. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AAC is highly prevalent, with approximately 1 in 10 adults over the age of 40 showing significant levels of hydroxyapatite build-up. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both claims report ~10% prevalence; the publication expresses this as prevalence in adults >40 but omits the 'despite low diabetes/CKD' clause, making the prevalence statement slightly rephrased.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Increased level of AAC is a strong prognostic indicator of cardiovascular outcomes for stenosis of precerebral arteries, Myocardial Infarction, and Ischemic Heart Disease. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Presentation of AAC is strongly predictive of future cardiovascular events, including stenosis of precerebral arteries, myocardial infarction, and ischemic heart disease. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the strong predictive relationship between AAC and the listed cardiovascular outcomes with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AAC is genetically correlated with cardiovascular-related traits, with genetic signals enriched in vascular and adipose tissue. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We identified three novel loci associated with abdominal aortic calcification (AAC): FGF9, NAV9, and APOE. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint emphasizes genetic correlation and tissue enrichment; the publication reports specific novel loci (a more concrete genetic result) with higher certainty, altering the nature of the genetic claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Elevated but clinically normal levels of serum phosphate and glycated hemoglobin are linked to increased vascular calcification. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim corresponds to this biochemical association; the claim present in the preprint does not appear in the listed publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AAC is an LDL-independent risk factor for cardiovascular outcomes, with risk similar to elevated LDL. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The risk for myocardial infarction from elevated AAC is comparable to the risk of hypercholesterolemia, yet most individuals who develop AAC are not hypercholesterolemic. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the comparable-risk assertion but adds nuance about most AAC patients not being hypercholesterolemic and downgrades certainty, refining the original conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Population-weighted density (PWD), some 'stay at home' metrics, monthly temperature and precipitation, race/ethnicity, and chronic low-respiratory death rate were all statistically significant in relation to COVID-19 deaths in the USA. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Population-weighted population density (PWPD), some 'stay at home' metrics, monthly temperature and precipitation, race/ethnicity, and chronic low-respiratory death rate were all statistically significant factors affecting COVID-19 deaths in the USA. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same set of statistically significant factors with equivalent certainty; wording differs only slightly (PWD vs PWPD).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PWD and mobility metrics dominated the factors impacting COVID-19 deaths, suggesting that the biggest impact was a function of where individuals lived rather than their actions. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PWPD and mobility metrics dominated the impact on COVID-19 deaths, suggesting that the biggest impact was a function of where individuals lived rather than their actions. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about dominance of density and mobility metrics is restated unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The loosening of 'lock-down' orders should be tailored to the local PWD to effectively manage COVID-19 deaths. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The loosening of 'lock-down' orders should be tailored to the local PWPD to effectively manage COVID-19 transmission. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication changes the target outcome from managing deaths to managing transmission; the policy recommendation remains but focuses on a different (narrower) epidemiological endpoint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Race/ethnicity, health, and climate effects could only account for a few percent of the variability in COVID-19 deaths, despite being statistically significant. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Race/ethnicity, health, and climate effects could only account for a few percent of the variability in COVID-19 deaths, despite being statistically significant. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The reported small explanatory contribution of those factors is presented identically with the same certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Limitations in the parameters chosen may mask contributions that could otherwise be present in the analysis of COVID-19 deaths. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Limitations in the parameters chosen may mask contributions from associations that were anticipated but not found. (certainty=low, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The limitation is restated with slightly different wording and the publication reports lower certainty, making the caveat more conservatively expressed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Functional connectomes consistently outperform structural connectomes as features for MDD classification across phenotypes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Functional connectomes outperformed structural connectomes as features for MDD classification across phenotypes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The core result that functional outperforms structural connectomes is retained verbatim with the same certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The highest accuracy of 61.06% was achieved when predicting the Currently Depressed phenotype using partial correlation functional connectomes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The highest accuracy of 64.8% was achieved in the Currently Depressed sample with additional CTQ criterion using partial correlation functional connectomes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a different (higher) top accuracy and explicitly ties it to an additional CTQ inclusion criterion, altering the original numeric claim and narrowing the condition for the result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Accuracy of classifying Currently Depressed participants with added CTQ threshold criterion rose to 65.74%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The highest accuracy of 64.8% was achieved in the Currently Depressed sample with additional CTQ criterion using partial correlation functional connectomes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both claims concern accuracy with an added CTQ criterion, but the numeric value differs slightly between preprint and publication, so the claim was revised (value adjusted).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There are neurobiological differences between MDD patients with and without childhood adversity. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There are neurobiological differences between MDD patients with and without childhood adversity. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about neurobiological differences by childhood adversity is reported identically with the same certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Differences in sensorimotor and visual subnetworks may serve as important biomarkers of MDD. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Differences in sensorimotor and visual subnetworks may serve as potential biomarkers of MDD. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same biomarker-oriented claim but expresses it with lower certainty (potential biomarkers) compared to the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Kidney function is the primary variable predicting circulating TMAO levels in adults, following age. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Kidney function is the primary variable predicting circulating TMAO levels, with age being the next most significant factor. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same ordering (kidney function then age) with equivalent certainty and meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a causal relationship between TMAO and kidney function decline that strengthens at more severe stages of cardiometabolic disease. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a causal relationship between TMAO and kidney function, supported by preclinical models showing that TMAO exposure increases kidney scarring. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains a causal link but frames it via preclinical evidence and omits the claim that the relationship strengthens with more severe cardiometabolic disease, narrowing the scope and tempering direct clinical inference.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TMAO exposure aggravates kidney fibrosis through ERK1/2 hyperactivation and synergistic TGF-β1 signaling. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a causal relationship between TMAO and kidney function, supported by preclinical models showing that TMAO exposure increases kidney scarring. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The mechanistic pathway (ERK1/2 and TGF-β1) stated in the preprint is not reported in the publication, which instead reports general preclinical evidence of scarring, thus removing mechanistic detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Patients receiving next-generation glucose-lowering drugs with reno-protective properties have significantly lower circulating TMAO levels compared to matched controls. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients receiving glucose-lowering drugs with reno-protective properties have significantly lower circulating TMAO compared to matched control individuals. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same comparative result with similar wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Lower TMAO levels in individuals medicated with reno-protective anti-diabetic drugs suggest a clinically actionable intervention for reducing TMAO-associated cardiovascular risk. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The analyses reveal a bidirectional relationship between kidney function and TMAO that can potentially be modified by reno-protective anti-diabetic drugs. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication couches the finding more cautiously as potential modification of the kidney–TMAO relationship, rather than asserting a clinically actionable intervention to reduce cardiovascular risk, narrowing and tempering the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We identified a predictive 24-gene signature to determine BRCAness. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We identified a 24-gene signature that predicts BRCAness. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The published claim restates the same 24-gene predictive signature for BRCAness with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Comprehensive analysis of the tumor microenvironment allowed us to identify patient samples with BRCAness and high immune infiltration. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Comprehensive immunogenomic analyses across patient cohorts identified samples with BRCAness and high immune infiltration. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same immunogenomic identification of BRCAness samples with high immune infiltration.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PARPi activated the cGAS-STING signaling pathway and the downstream innate immune response in a similar manner to HGSOC patients with BRCAness status. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the PARP inhibitor activation of cGAS-STING or direct equivalence to patient immunophenotypes; this mechanistic/experimental claim is absent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Genomic instability in HGSOC affects the tumor immune environment, and TAMs play a crucial role in modulating the immune response. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Genomic instability in HGSOC affects the tumor immune environment, and TAMs play a crucial role in modulating the immune response. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The published claim repeats the same conclusion but with elevated stated certainty (medium → high), representing an amplification of confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We have developed a diagnostic application that uses RNA sequencing data to predict vulnerability and response to combination immunotherapy. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We have developed a diagnostic application that uses RNA sequencing data to predict vulnerability and response to combination immunotherapy. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same diagnostic application claim unchanged in content and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Depression at age 24 was associated with both altered concentrations of immuno-metabolic markers and increased extreme-valued inflammatory markers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Depression at age 24 was associated with both altered concentrations of immuno-metabolic markers and increased extreme-valued inflammatory markers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same association at age 24 with the same emphasis and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Immuno-metabolic biomarkers predicted depression diagnosis better than chance, with a Balanced Accuracy of 0.580. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Immuno-metabolic biomarkers predicted depression diagnosis and depression with high somatic symptoms better than chance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the predictive result but extends it to also highlight prediction of depression with high somatic symptoms; the exact metric (BA=0.580) is not repeated.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The relationships between affective symptoms and blood immunometabolic biomarkers indicate two biotypes of depressive symptoms: somatic-inflamed and anxious-non-inflamed. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Inflammatory and metabolic biomarkers show distinct, opposing associations with somatic and anxiety symptoms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports distinct opposing biomarker associations with symptom domains but stops short of explicitly defining two biotypes, thus toning down the categorical biotype claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Higher somatic-inflamed component scores are associated with greater depressive symptom severity over the next five years. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Individuals with higher somatic-inflammatory component scores had greater depressive symptoms severity over the next five years. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The longitudinal association with higher somatic-inflammatory component scores and future symptom severity is reported equivalently.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Immuno-metabolic biomarkers show varying, opposing associations with somatic, mood, and anxiety symptoms. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Inflammatory and metabolic biomarkers show distinct, opposing associations with somatic and anxiety symptoms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes opposing associations with somatic and anxiety symptoms but does not explicitly discuss mood symptoms, narrowing the original phrasing.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Alterations in immuno-metabolic homeostasis are present in young adults with depression well before the typical age of onset of cardiometabolic diseases. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This conclusion about the timing of immuno-metabolic alterations relative to cardiometabolic disease onset does not appear in the preprint claims and is newly stated in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SuStaIn identified two unique subtypes of brain atrophy progression: a 'cortical-first' subtype and a 'subcortical-first' subtype. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SuStaIn identified two distinct subtypes of brain atrophy progression in iRBD: a 'cortical-first' subtype and a 'subcortical-first' subtype. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both versions report the same two-subtype finding with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Patients classified to either subtype had higher motor and cognitive disease burden and were more likely to phenoconvert to overt disease compared with those that were not classifiable. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both cortical- and subcortical-first subtypes were associated with a higher rate of increase in MDS-UPDRS-III scores over time. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the association with greater motor burden (MDS-UPDRS-III) for both subtypes, but is more specific about motor score trajectories; the broader statement about phenoconversion likelihood is reported elsewhere in a subtype-specific manner.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Of the 84 iRBD patients who developed overt disease during follow-up, those with a subcortical-first pattern of atrophy were more likely to phenoconvert at earlier SuStaIn stages, particularly to a parkinsonism phenotype. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the specific finding that subcortical-first patients phenoconvert earlier to a parkinsonism phenotype; instead it emphasizes different subtype-specific outcomes, so this specific result is absent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Later disease stages in both subtypes were associated with more imminent phenoconversion to a dementia phenotype. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cognitive decline was subtype-specific, being associated with advancing stages in patients classified as cortical-first but not in those classified as subcortical-first. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint's claim that later stages in both subtypes predict imminent dementia is revised in the publication to a subtype-specific association (cortical-first only), narrowing the original generalization.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This demonstrates insights into underlying disease biology and the potential value of categorizing patients in clinical trials. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly state the general translational conclusion about clinical trials in the claims provided, so the preprint's explicit trial-focused conclusion is not present.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The cortical-first subtype is linked to an increased likelihood of developing dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) compared to Parkinson's disease (PD). (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This specific conclusion associating the cortical-first subtype with DLB vs PD is newly emphasized in the publication and is not stated in the preprint claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients classified within the cortical-first subtype had a significantly increased likelihood of phenoconversion compared to stage 0/non-classified patients. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication highlights a subtype-specific increased phenoconversion likelihood (cortical-first) not explicitly claimed in the preprint, making this a new, more specific publication claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We identified three models to predict hospital admission from pneumonia and other events as a proxy for COVID-19 pneumonia in the general population. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The review identified seven models for identifying people at risk in the general population and 118 diagnostic models for detecting covid-19. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports different (larger) counts of models identified in the general population compared with the preprint, representing a revision and broadening of the scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We identified 18 diagnostic models for COVID-19 detection in symptomatic patients, 13 of which were machine learning models based on CT images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The review identified seven models for identifying people at risk in the general population and 118 diagnostic models for detecting covid-19. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a much larger total of diagnostic models (118) than the preprint's 18, indicating a revision in the counts and a broader review scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Included studies were poorly reported, compromising their subsequent appraisal and recommendation for use in daily practice. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Almost all published prediction models are poorly reported and at high risk of bias, making their reported predictive performance probably optimistic. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication echoes the limitation that studies are poorly reported and at high risk of bias, consistent with the preprint's appraisal.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: All studies were appraised at high risk of bias, raising concern that the models may be flawed and perform poorly when applied in practice. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Almost all published prediction models are poorly reported and at high risk of bias, making their reported predictive performance probably optimistic. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates that most models are at high risk of bias and that reported performance is likely optimistic, matching the preprint limitations.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed models are poorly reported and at high risk of bias, suggesting their reported performance is likely optimistic. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Almost all published prediction models are poorly reported and at high risk of bias, making their reported predictive performance probably optimistic. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same conclusion but with stronger stated certainty (medium → high), amplifying confidence in the critique.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Reported C index estimates from the strongest form of validation available per model ranged from 0.71 to 0.99 in prediction models for the general population. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The specific summary of C index ranges is new in the publication and was not present among the preprint claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The Jehi diagnostic model and the 4C mortality score were identified as promising models that should soon be validated in multiple cohorts. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Identification of specific promising models (Jehi and 4C) for further validation is an addition in the published claims not present in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Methodological guidance should be followed because unreliable predictions could cause more harm than benefit in guiding clinical decisions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The explicit recommendation to follow methodological guidance to avoid harm is newly stated in the publication and not in the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The three Clade VI isolates were separated by more than 36,000 SNPs from all existing C. auris clades.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The three investigated C auris isolates were separated by at least 37,000 SNPs from all existing C auris clades.
alignments.rationale: The publication refines the numeric separation (≥37,000 vs >36,000) but conveys the same result about large SNP distance, so the claim is clarified rather than changed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The discovery of a new C. auris clade in Singapore and Bangladesh highlights the unknown genetic diversity and origin of C. auris.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The discovery of a new C auris clade highlights the unknown genetic diversity and origin of C auris in under-resourced regions.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the main conclusion about unknown diversity/origin but frames it more broadly (under-resourced regions) rather than naming Singapore/Bangladesh, broadening the scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: High-resolution SNP analysis revealed six distinct genetic clusters representing the five known clades and the new sixth clade.
alignments.pub_claim_text: These isolates represent a new clade of C auris, designated as clade VI.
alignments.rationale: The preprint result about six clusters is carried into the publication as the designation of the new clade (clade VI); the publication emphasizes the clade designation while preserving the underlying finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A machine learning approach can be used to flag outlier genomes for further investigations, providing early alerts for potential new public health threats.
alignments.rationale: The publication's claims do not mention a machine learning approach or outlier-flagging; this claim from the preprint is not present in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Active surveillance and continued vigilance are necessary due to the potential circulation of yet-to-be-reported strains in Southeast Asia and South Asia.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly assert the need for active surveillance in those regions; the preprint's specific surveillance recommendation is not reflected in the listed publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Patients with Parkinson disease (PD) and dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) generally have worse olfaction than healthy controls.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients with Parkinson’s disease (PD) or dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) show markedly worse olfaction than healthy controls.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result using similar language and emphasis; there is no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A simplified scent identification test using only seven specific odorants can effectively distinguish PD/DLB subjects from healthy controls.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A subset of 7 shared odorants can distinguish PD/DLB patients from healthy controls with similar effectiveness to traditional smell tests.
alignments.rationale: Both statements assert that a 7-odorant subset effectively discriminates PD/DLB from controls; the publication frames it relative to traditional tests but does not change the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Increased age is associated with a decline in olfaction.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Distractors and age play a critical role in olfaction performance.
alignments.rationale: The publication expands the preprint's observation by emphasizing age (and distractors) as critical factors, strengthening the role of age in olfaction performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Males generally score lower than females in olfaction tests, although this finding is not significant across all cohorts.
alignments.rationale: No publication claim mentions sex differences in olfaction; the preprint's male-female finding is not reflected in the listed publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The discrimination of PD/DLB subjects from age-matched controls is more accurate than that of PD/DLB from PSP/MSA patients.
alignments.rationale: The publication claims do not discuss comparisons between PD/DLB discrimination versus PSP/MSA, so this specific comparative conclusion is absent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The patient-facing digital symptom and history taking tool was endorsed as useful in facilitating conversation by 75% of patients, 73% of physicians, and 100% of nurses.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The digital tool was strongly endorsed by patients and clinicians for facilitating conversation in the emergency department.
alignments.rationale: The publication summarizes the quantitative endorsements as strong endorsement by patients and clinicians, so the detailed percentages are condensed but the finding is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Nurses judged the tool as potentially time saving, while physicians assessed it as time saving only in some ED medical specialisms.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Most nurses judged the tool as potentially time-saving, while physicians agreed only for a subset of medical specialties.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this distinction between nurses and physicians nearly verbatim; there is no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The system has clear potential to improve patient-healthcare professional interaction and make efficiency savings in the emergency department.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The tool has the potential to improve patient-health care provider interaction and contribute to emergency department efficiency savings.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same conclusion about potential improvements to interaction and ED efficiency; wording is equivalent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Acceptability of the tool was high, with improved rapport experienced 90% of the time for patients, 73% for physicians, and 100% for nurses.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients reported high usability of the tool and understood its questions effectively.
alignments.rationale: The publication focuses on patient-reported usability and understanding and omits the detailed multi-stakeholder acceptability percentages, narrowing the original multi-group acceptability claim to patients.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Further research and testing is required before time savings can be consistently delivered to ED clinicians across the range of relevant ED medical specialisms.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The pilot study confirmed the feasibility of conducting a larger study in the emergency department setting.
alignments.rationale: Both statements emphasize the need for further study; the publication frames this as confirmation of feasibility for larger studies, clarifying the next step.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is significant county-level geographical variation in obesity prevalence in the United States.
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is significant county-level geographical variation in obesity prevalence in the United States, explained by complex sociodemographic and environmental factors.
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates the same core finding and adds explanation about contributing factors, but the primary claim about geographic variation is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CART identified 11 clusters with a 60.8% relative increase in obesity prevalence across the spectrum.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The CART method identified 11 clusters of counties with a 60.8% relative increase in obesity prevalence across the spectrum.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this methodological result verbatim; there is no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Seven key SEDH variables were identified by CART to guide the categorization of clusters.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Seven key sociodemographic and environmental determinants of health were identified as significant in guiding the categorization of obesity clusters.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same result with synonymous wording (SEDH -> sociodemographic and environmental determinants); the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The use of ML techniques to analyze SEDH factors can provide valuable insights into the importance of these upstream determinants of obesity.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine-learning techniques can provide valuable insights into upstream determinants of obesity and aid in developing geo-specific strategic interventions.
alignments.rationale: Both preprint and publication make the same conclusion about the value of ML techniques in elucidating upstream determinants; the publication additionally links this to intervention planning but does not change the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings aid in the development of geo-specific strategic interventions to help battle the obesity pandemic.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine-learning techniques can provide valuable insights into upstream determinants of obesity and aid in developing geo-specific strategic interventions.
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly connects ML-derived insights to geo-specific intervention development, which matches the preprint's stated application of the findings.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The GECCOS study aims to identify germline genetic variants associated with acute and late complications of childhood cancer.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The GECCOS study aims to improve knowledge of these genetic variants to individualize cancer treatment and follow-up care.
alignments.rationale: Both state the study's aim; the publication emphasizes the ultimate goal (improving knowledge to individualize care) while preserving the study's focus on germline variants associated with complications.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Knowledge of germline genetic variants associated with childhood cancer-associated health conditions will help to further individualize cancer treatment and follow-up care.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Identifying genetic variants may lead to improved efficacy and reduced side effects in childhood cancer treatment.
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses a similar anticipated benefit (improved efficacy/reduced side effects) that aligns with the preprint's point about individualizing treatment and follow-up, clarifying potential outcomes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study will perform association analyses to identify genetic variants associated with specified health conditions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Germline genetic variants are associated with acute and late complications of childhood cancer.
alignments.rationale: The preprint described planned association analyses; the publication asserts the presence of associations, representing a shift from describing planned methods to reporting results (hence revised and an increase in claim strength).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GECCOS will enable genotype-phenotype association analyses on complications associated with childhood cancer and its treatments.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The GECCOS study aims to improve knowledge of these genetic variants to individualize cancer treatment and follow-up care.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the enabling role of GECCOS as part of its aim to improve knowledge for individualized care; the contribution is restated in a more outcome-oriented phrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A wearable sensor array can detect residual motor unit activity in paralyzed muscles after severe cervical spinal cord injury.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study provides the first demonstration of a wearable interface for recording and decoding firing rates of motor units below the level of injury in a person with motor complete SCI.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the wearable system as a first demonstration of recording/decoding motor unit firing below the injury, which clarifies and operationalizes the preprint's detection claim without changing its meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Volitional recruitment of motor neurons below the level of injury was observed across attempted movements of individual fingers and overt wrist and elbow movements.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Volitional recruitment of motor units below the level of injury was observed across attempted movements of individual fingers and overt wrist and elbow movements.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result verbatim, so the claim is unchanged in meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Single digit movement intentions were classified offline from the EMG power or motor unit firing rates with median classification accuracies greater than 75%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Single digit movement intentions were classified offline from the electromyogram (EMG) power or motor unit firing rates with median classification accuracies greater than 75%.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the offline classification accuracies equivalently, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The online decomposition algorithm extracted motor units in 1.2 ms, and the firing rates predicted the correct digit motion 88 ± 24% of the time.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The online decomposition algorithm extracted motor units in 1.2 ms, and the firing rates predicted the correct digit motion 88 ± 24% of the time.
alignments.rationale: This technical performance claim is reproduced exactly in the publication, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study provides the first demonstration of a wearable interface for recording and decoding firing rates of motor neurons below the level of injury in a person with tetraplegia after motor complete SCI.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study provides the first demonstration of a wearable interface for recording and decoding firing rates of motor units below the level of injury in a person with motor complete SCI.
alignments.rationale: The publication makes the same novelty claim about a first wearable interface for recording/decoding motor unit firing below injury; wording is effectively equivalent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Higher WMH burden increases stroke severity in case of stroke lesions involving left-hemispheric insular, opercular and inferior frontal regions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Higher white matter hyperintensity (WMH) burden is associated with increased stroke severity in cases of stroke lesions involving left-hemispheric insular, opercular, and inferior frontal regions.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this region-specific interaction between WMH burden and stroke severity with essentially the same wording and strength.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Higher WMH burden is associated with higher odds of unfavorable outcomes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Higher WMH burden increases the odds of unfavorable outcomes independent of lesion location.
alignments.rationale: The publication strengthens the preprint association by asserting the effect is independent of lesion location, which broadens scope and increases confidence in the association.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Bilateral subcortical regions were most relevant in explaining long term unfavorable outcome.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report or emphasize a claim that bilateral subcortical regions are most relevant for long-term unfavorable outcome; this specific regional claim is absent from the paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Three distinct lesion patterns substantially explained acute stroke severity across all patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Lesions centered on left-hemispheric insular, opercular, and inferior frontal regions and right-hemispheric temporoparietal regions have more pronounced effects on stroke severity in cases of high compared with low WMH burden.
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies which lesion patterns are most consequential and frames their interaction with WMH burden, clarifying the preprint's broader statement about distinct lesion patterns.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We constructed a 263 marker panel from filtering genome-wide association scans of the NMR in each study.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We constructed a 263 marker panel from filtering genome-wide association scans of the nicotine metabolism ratio (NMR) in four multiethnic studies.
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies the scope by specifying the four multiethnic studies used, but the core contribution (a 263 marker panel from GWAS filtering) is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The correlations between the observed and predicted NMR ranged from 0.69 to 0.97 depending on the model.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The correlations between the observed and predicted NMR ranged from 0.69 to 0.97 depending on the machine learning model used.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this quantitative model performance claim with equivalent wording and meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ensemble model generalizes well out-of-sample across ancestries, with correlations of: 0.52 for African ancestry, 0.61 for Asian ancestry, and 0.46 for European ancestry.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ensemble model generalizes well in validation studies across ancestries, with correlations of 0.52 for African ancestry, 0.61 for Asian ancestry, and 0.46 for European ancestry.
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the same validation correlations across ancestries, keeping the claim intact.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The most influential predictors of NMR identified in more than two models were rs56113850, rs11878604, and 21 other genetic variants near CYP2A6 as well as age and ancestry.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The most influential predictors of NMR identified were genetic variants near CYP2A6, age, and ancestry.
alignments.rationale: The publication summarizes the influential predictors more generally (variants near CYP2A6, age, ancestry) and omits the specific SNP list, narrowing the claim's detail while preserving its substance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The knowledge of how an individual metabolizes nicotine could be used to help select the optimal path to reducing or quitting tobacco use.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed models could help select the optimal path to reducing or quitting tobacco use and evaluating risks of tobacco use.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the translational implication about selecting cessation strategies and broadens it to include evaluating risks of tobacco use, expanding the potential applications.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There was a small positive causal effect of SEMLS on ΔGDI (1.68 GDI points).
alignments.pub_claim_text: There was a small positive causal effect of SEMLS on ΔGDI (1.70 GDI points).
alignments.rationale: The publication reports effectively the same small causal effect with a negligible numerical difference, clarifying the estimate but not changing the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Altered motor control and strength had strong effects on GDI pre, but minimal effects on ΔGDI.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Altered motor control and strength had strong effects on GDI<sub>pre</sub>, but minimal effects on ΔGDI.
alignments.rationale: This result is restated in the publication with equivalent meaning and emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Altered motor control and other baseline impairments do have a causal effect on a child’s gait pattern but minimal effect on expected changes in GDI after SEMLS.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Altered motor control did have a strong effect on GDI<sub>pre</sub>, indicating that these impairments do have a causal effect on a child's gait pattern.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the pre-surgery effect on gait (GDI_pre) to make explicit the causal interpretation of baseline impairments on gait pattern, clarifying the preprint's conclusion about causal effects.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Heterogeneity in outcomes suggests there are other factors contributing to changes in gait.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Heterogeneity in outcomes suggests there are other factors contributing to changes in gait.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains this conclusion about heterogeneity and unmeasured contributors without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AD-NeuroScore was significantly associated with diagnosis and all disease severity scores at baseline.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AD-NeuroScore is significantly associated with diagnosis and disease severity scores at baseline.
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the baseline association claim with equivalent wording and strength.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Associations between AD-NeuroScore and disease severity (CDR-SB and ADAS-11) were significantly stronger than with adjusted hippocampal volume (AHV).
alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance of AD-NeuroScore is equivalent or superior to adjusted hippocampal volume in AD research.
alignments.rationale: The publication rephrases the comparison to AHV as equivalent or superior rather than asserting significantly stronger associations, which softens the original claim's strength.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Baseline AD-NeuroScore was associated with change in diagnosis and changes in disease severity scores at all time points.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Baseline AD-NeuroScore is associated with changes in diagnosis and disease severity scores at all time points with available data.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the longitudinal association claim, adding a minor qualifier about available data but not changing the substantive result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Performance of AD-NeuroScore was equivalent, or in some cases superior, to AHV.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance of AD-NeuroScore is equivalent or superior to adjusted hippocampal volume in AD research.
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the comparative performance claim and presents it with higher certainty (publication labels it high confidence), effectively amplifying confidence in this finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These early validation results suggest that AD-NeuroScore has the potential to be a clinically meaningful biomarker for dementia.
alignments.pub_claim_text: AD-NeuroScore shows promising results in detecting Alzheimer's disease, benchmarking disease severity, and predicting disease progression.
alignments.rationale: The publication echoes the preprint's cautious conclusion that AD-NeuroScore is promising and potentially clinically meaningful, restating the intended applications.

alignments.pub_claim_text: AD-NeuroScore is clinically practical and interpretable compared to other metrics.
alignments.rationale: The publication includes a new claim about clinical practicality and interpretability that was not present in the preprint; this is an added evaluative assertion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A combination of personality, anxiety, and depression scores provided the highest predictive accuracy for classifying participants across cognitive states. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Mean predictive accuracy across all participant groups was highest when utilizing a combination of personality, depression, and anxiety scores. (certainty=high, type=result)
