alignments.rationale: The wording and scope are effectively identical, both stating that the combination of personality/depression/anxiety yields the highest predictive accuracy across groups.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CSF biomarkers, personality, depression, anxiety, and ApoE genotype show complementary value for classification of Alzheimer's disease and its at-risk stages. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CSF biomarkers, personality, depression, anxiety, and ApoE genotype show complementary value for classification of Alzheimer's disease and its at-risk stages. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same conclusion listing the same feature sets and their complementary value.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Established and candidate predictors had limited success in classifying subjective cognitive decline and amnestic mild cognitive impairment. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Classification of participants with subjective cognitive decline or amnestic mild cognitive impairment was near chance level for all assessed feature sets. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides a more specific and stronger characterization ('near chance level') of the limited success noted in the preprint, clarifying the extent of the limitation.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Healthy controls were best predicted by a feature set comprised of depression and anxiety scores. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This subgroup-specific result (HCs best predicted by depression/anxiety) is presented in the publication but not stated in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Participants with Alzheimer's disease were best predicted by a feature set containing cerebrospinal fluid biomarkers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This AD-specific finding appears in the publication but was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Safe Blues provides a privacy-preserving digital alternative to studying the spread of COVID-19. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Safe Blues addresses the problem of delayed infection measurements through online measurements and machine learning methods. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the contribution to emphasize addressing delayed measurements via online/ML methods rather than explicitly highlighting 'privacy-preserving' as the primary contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Data about the spread of Safe Blues is uploaded to a central server in real time, offering a more up-to-date picture than actual COVID-19 data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Safe Blues addresses the problem of delayed infection measurements through online measurements and machine learning methods. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the online measurement idea but omits the explicit claim about a central server and the direct superiority to actual COVID-19 data, narrowing and tempering the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sampling of Safe Blues data is not biased by being applied only to individuals who have shown symptoms or who have come into contact with known positive cases. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not report or reiterate this specific sampling-bias claim, so it appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Information from Safe Blues could aid health authorities in making informed decisions regarding social distancing and other measures in the medium term and end game of COVID-19. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly make the policy-utility claim for health authorities found in the preprint, so this forward-looking conclusion is not present among the publication claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The relationship between counts of multiple forms of the Safe Blues strands and the progress of the actual epidemic is exploited in the Safe Blues approach. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This methodological/result claim about exploiting strand-count relationships appears in the publication but was not present in the preprint claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: An experimental minimal viable product of Safe Blues has been developed and presented as an app on Android devices. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The app/MVP development is reported in the publication but was not in the preprint claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: A university-wide experiment of Safe Blues is being prepared following initial exploration via simulation experiments. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication mentions preparation of a university-wide experiment, which was not claimed in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The SelectStitch framework enhances the diagnostic capabilities of composite images derived from otoscope videos compared to base composite images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The SelectStitch framework demonstrates greater diagnostic capabilities compared to base composite images as graded by three ENT specialists. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result that SelectStitch improves diagnostic capability as graded by ENT specialists.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In the first round of assessments, ENT specialists graded improvement for 58, 57, and 71 composite images out of 100 for SelectStitch over the base composite. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: In the first round of assessments, ENT specialists graded improvement for 58, 57, and 71 composite images out of 100 for SelectStitch over the base composite. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This numerical result is reproduced verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Only 6%, 3%, and 3% of the cases received a lesser score than the base composite images in Round-1, indicating a high level of diagnostic consistency with SelectStitch. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Only 6%, 3%, and 3% of the cases received a lesser score than the base composite images in Round-1 for ENT-I, ENT-II, and ENT-III, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats these percentages and the implication of rare worse performance, matching the preprint result (publication wording is slightly more specific about raters).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Frame selection improves the diagnostic quality of composite images from otoscope video clips. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The frame selection and stitching process increases the probability of detecting a lesion even if it appears in a few frames. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies the mechanistic benefit (increased probability of lesion detection) while preserving the conclusion that frame selection improves diagnostic quality.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We created CONSORT-TM consisting of 10,709 sentences, 4,845 (45%) of which were annotated with 5,246 labels. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The annotated corpus, CONSORT-TM, contains more fine-grained information than earlier RCT corpora. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the corpus contribution qualitatively (more fine-grained than prior corpora) rather than restating exact counts; this is a clarification/reframing of the corpus description.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model based on BioBERT performed best overall for recognizing methodology-related items with a micro-precision of 0.82. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model based on BioBERT performed best overall for recognizing methodology-related items with a micro-precision of 0.82. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the model performance result identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Low frequency of some CONSORT items made it difficult to train effective text mining models to recognize them. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Low frequency of some CONSORT items made it difficult to train effective text mining models to recognize them. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: This limitation is stated the same way in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: For the items commonly reported, CONSORT-TM can serve as a testbed for text mining methods that assess RCT transparency, rigor, and reliability. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not explicitly include this forward-looking statement about serving as a testbed, so it appears omitted from the provided publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Minor modifications to the annotation scheme and a larger corpus could facilitate improved text mining models. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not restate this recommendation for annotation-scheme changes or corpus expansion among the provided claims; instead it reports different follow-up results.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Combining models using majority vote and label aggregation further improved precision and recall. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes an ensemble-modeling result (majority vote/label aggregation improves performance) that was not present in the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MMSE scores correlated significantly with reaction times and two EEG biomarkers: A0 and ST4. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: MMSE scores significantly correlated with reaction times and EEG features A0 and ST4. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same correlation result between MMSE, reaction times, and A0/ST4 features.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Both biomarkers A0 and ST4 showed significant separation between study groups, indicating different cognitive patterns between different stages of cognitive decline. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The features A0 and ST4 showed significant separation between study groups based on MMSE scores. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same result about A0 and ST4 separating study groups (based on MMSE) as in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study successfully demonstrated the ability to assess cognitive states using a portable single-channel EEG device with an interactive cognitive assessment. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study successfully demonstrated the ability to assess cognitive states with an easy-to-use single-channel EEG using an auditory cognitive assessment. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the conclusion but specifies the cognitive assessment modality ('auditory') rather than the more general term 'interactive', thus clarifying the method.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Future studies should explore the potential usefulness of this tool in characterizing changes in EEG patterns of cognitive decline over time. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication's presented claims do not include this future-direction recommendation, so it appears omitted in the provided publication claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: EEG features Theta, Delta, A0, and VC9 showed increased activity with higher cognitive load levels, indicating different activity patterns between young and senior participants. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This additional result on Theta/Delta/VC9 activity and cognitive load / age-group differences appears in the publication but was not present among the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our multivariable models were able to predict the probability of inpatient mortality with an F1 score of 83.7%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed probabilistic models were able to predict the probability of inpatient mortality, required ventilatory support, and duration of inpatient treatment for COVID-19 adult inpatients.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the claim that mortality can be predicted but omits the specific F1 performance and groups the outcome with others, so the claim is substantively revised and broadened to multiple outcomes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictive models can estimate the level of ventilatory support required, with F1 scores ranging from 55.8% to 71.5%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed probabilistic models were able to predict the probability of inpatient mortality, required ventilatory support, and duration of inpatient treatment for COVID-19 adult inpatients.
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms prediction of ventilatory support but drops the reported F1 ranges, so the core claim remains but performance metrics were removed (revision).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The models can predict the duration of inpatient treatment, with accuracy varying from 46.7% to 69.8%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed probabilistic models were able to predict the probability of inpatient mortality, required ventilatory support, and duration of inpatient treatment for COVID-19 adult inpatients.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the claim that duration can be predicted but omits the specific accuracy range, so the substantive content is revised (metrics removed).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our findings demonstrate reliable, multivariable predictive models for four outcomes that utilize readily available clinical information for COVID-19 adult inpatients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our findings demonstrate reliable, multivariable, quantitative predictive models for four outcomes using readily available clinical information.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion about reliable multivariable models for four outcomes with essentially the same scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Further research is required to externally validate our models and demonstrate their utility as clinical decision-making tools.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Further research is required to externally validate our models and demonstrate their utility as risk stratification and clinical decision-making tools.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the same limitation and adds explicit mention of 'risk stratification' as a use case, so the statement is clarified/expanded without changing its core meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Waning antibody levels lead to reduced sensitivity of serological diagnostic tests over time.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not assert that waning causes reduced sensitivity; this explicit causal claim is not present in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: One year after symptoms, 36% of anti-S IgG, 31% of anti-RBD IgG, and 7% of anti-N IgG remain.
alignments.pub_claim_text: One year after symptoms, we estimate that 36% of anti-Spike immunoglobulin G (IgG) remains.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same 36% estimate for anti-Spike IgG but omits the other isotype/antigen figures, so the claim is narrowed/clarified to anti-Spike IgG.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The diagnostic test classified previous infections with 99% specificity and 98% sensitivity, with no evidence for declining sensitivity over the time scale considered.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the specific 99%/98% performance figures nor the explicit 'no evidence for declining sensitivity' statement, so this specific performance claim is not present in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Quantitative measurements of antibody responses were used to train machine learning algorithms for classification of previous infection and estimation of time since infection.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The multiplex assay classified previous infections into time intervals of 0-3 months, 3-6 months, and 6-12 months.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the outcome of classification into time intervals (implying the method), but does not emphasize the training procedure; the methodological claim is represented more as a result (clarified).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The diagnostic test provided accurate classification of time since infection into intervals of 0 – 3 months, 3 – 6 months, and 6 – 12 months.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The multiplex assay classified previous infections into time intervals of 0-3 months, 3-6 months, and 6-12 months.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this classification result verbatim, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Counties with low socioeconomic status (SES) were important drivers of SARS-CoV-2 infections and COVID-19 deaths during the second wave in Germany.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Counties with low socioeconomic status (SES) had higher infection and death rates.
alignments.rationale: The publication states the same result that low-SES counties had higher infection and death rates, matching the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: High international migration, a high proportion of foreigners, and a large nursing home population were also significant factors associated with increased COVID-19 incidence and death rates.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Counties with high international migration, a high proportion of foreigners, and a large nursing home population also had higher infection and death rates.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats these associations directly, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proportion of the population that voted for the Alternative for Germany (AfD) party was correlated with high incidence and death rates during the period of intense exponential increase in infections.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proportion of the population that voted for the Alternative for Germany (AfD) party was correlated with high incidence and death rates during the period of intense exponential increase in infections.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this specific correlation, so it is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Risky working conditions with reduced opportunities for social distancing and a high chronic disease burden contribute to higher risks of SARS-CoV-2 infections and COVID-19 deaths in low-SES counties.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning approaches can reveal regional characteristics that are associated with high rates of infection and mortality.
alignments.rationale: The preprint attributes causes (risky work, chronic disease) while the publication presents a methodological conclusion about ML revealing regional characteristics; the causal detail is not reproduced, so the claim content is revised to a methodological statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Noncompliance with Corona measures and spill-over effects from neighbouring counties increased the spread of the virus.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not state these specific mechanisms (noncompliance and spill-over) as findings, so this preprint conclusion is not present in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Both Indian and European young type 2 diabetes patients were predominantly classified into severely insulin-deficient (SIDD) and mild obesity-related (MOD) subgroups.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Both Indian and European young-onset type 2 diabetes patients were predominantly classified into severe insulin-deficient (SIDD) and mild obesity-related (MOD) subgroups.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same classification finding for both cohorts, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In WellGen, SIDD (53%) was more common than MOD (38%), contrary to figures in Europeans.
alignments.pub_claim_text: In WellGen, SIDD (53%) was more common than MOD (38%), contrary to findings in Europeans.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same cohort prevalence comparison, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our data identified insulin deficiency as the major driver of type 2 diabetes in young Indians, unlike in young European patients in whom obesity and insulin resistance predominate.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our data identified insulin deficiency as the major driver of type 2 diabetes in young Indians, unlike in young European individuals in whom obesity and insulin resistance predominate.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same etiological conclusion contrasting Indian and European patients, so it is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Nephropathy and retinopathy were more prevalent in SIDD compared to MOD while the latter had higher prevalence of neuropathy.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Nephropathy and retinopathy were more prevalent in SIDD compared with MOD in the WellGen cohort.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the increased nephropathy/retinopathy in SIDD but omits the statement about higher neuropathy in MOD, so the result is partially revised (reduced detail).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our results provide useful clues to pathophysiological mechanisms and susceptibility to complications in young Indian type 2 diabetes, and suggest a need to review management strategies.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings suggest a need to review management strategies for type 2 diabetes in the young Indian population.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the recommendation to review management strategies but presents it with lower reported certainty; the core conclusion remains but confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The nasopharyngeal metabolome of COVID-19 infected individuals exhibits a unique metabolomic signature characterized by specific changes in small molecules.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study demonstrates that COVID-19 infection results in a unique nasopharyngeal metabolomic signature.
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly states the same finding that COVID-19 produces a unique nasopharyngeal metabolomic signature, matching the preprint claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LysoPCaC18:2 concentration is significantly increased in COVID-19 patients, while beta-hydroxybutyric acid, Met SO, succinic acid, and carnosine concentrations are significantly decreased.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Lysophosphatidylcholines-a-C18:2 concentration is significantly increased in COVID-19 patients, while concentrations of beta-hydroxybutyric acid, Methionine sulfoxide, succinic acid, and carnosine are significantly decreased.
alignments.rationale: The publication lists the same metabolite changes using equivalent nomenclature, so the result is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A machine learning model achieved an accuracy of 96% in categorizing patients with a viral infection based on the identified analytes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A machine learning model identified 28 discriminating analytes with an accuracy of 96% for categorizing patients with a viral infection.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the 96% accuracy but adds the detail that 28 analytes were discriminating, clarifying the original model description.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Carnosine is identified as a promising potential therapeutic target against SARS-CoV-2 due to its ability to interfere with the binding of SARS-CoV-2 to the ACE2 receptor.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Carnosine and LysoPCaC18:2 are identified as potential therapeutic targets for COVID-19.
alignments.rationale: The publication identifies carnosine as a potential therapeutic target but omits the proposed ACE2-binding mechanism, so the mechanistic claim was removed (revised).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study provides compelling evidence for the use of metabolomics in identifying novel drug targets for viral respiratory infections in the nasopharynx.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Carnosine and LysoPCaC18:2 are identified as potential therapeutic targets for COVID-19.
alignments.rationale: The publication narrows the broad contribution claim to specific candidate targets (carnosine, LysoPCaC18:2), clarifying the general claim about metabolomics identifying drug targets.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Recurrent participants showed significantly delayed recovery of microbial diversity and depletion of key anaerobic taxa at multiple time-points. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The amplicon sequencing data showed significantly delayed recovery of microbial diversity in recurrent participants. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements report the same result about delayed recovery of microbial diversity in recurrent participants from sequencing data with consistent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The metabolomic data indicated distinct functional abnormalities in the microbiome or host in recurrent participants. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The metabolomic data showed delayed recovery in recurrent participants and mapped to pathways suggesting distinct functional abnormalities in the microbiome or host. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds pathway mapping and raises the certainty, clarifying and strengthening the original metabolomics functional-abnormality claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The metabolomic data can accurately predict future CDI recurrence at one week and two weeks post-treatment. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We demonstrated that the metabolomic data can accurately predict future recurrence at 1 week and 2 weeks post-treatment for primary CDI. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the predictive performance but specifies it applies to primary CDI, narrowing the scope while keeping confidence high.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A small set of metabolites can serve as candidate metabolic biomarkers for diagnostics development related to CDI recurrence. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our findings have implications for the development of diagnostic tests and treatments that could ultimately short-circuit the cycle of CDI recurrence. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the preprint's specific candidate-metabolite claim as broader implications for diagnostics and treatments rather than explicitly naming a small set of metabolite biomarkers.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study identified 24,311 total posts related to STDs on Reddit during the COVID-19 pandemic. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's claims do not report the specific post count, so this numeric result from the preprint does not appear in the paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LDA Coding achieved high coherence values for the identified topics across different time periods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Latent Dirichlet Allocation topic modeling showed that with 8 topics for each time period, we achieved high coherence values. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication gives additional methodological detail (8 topics per period) while confirming the high coherence result from the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The relative proportion of STD-related topics on Reddit changed over time, with some new topics emerging. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The relative proportion of topics changed and new topics emerged over the course of the COVID-19 pandemic. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both versions report the same observation about changing topic proportions and emergence of new topics over the pandemic period.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Reduction in STD screening during the pandemic may lead to negative health outcomes due to missed cases. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Reduction in STD screening may result in observed negative health outcomes due to missed cases, which also impacts onward transmission. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the consequence of onward transmission, broadening the public-health implications of reduced screening described in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Reddit provides a novel and anonymous platform for discussing sensitive STD-related topics, enhancing understanding of sexual behaviors and health engagement. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study provides evidence in favor of establishing Reddit as having feasibility and utility to enhance the understanding of sexual behaviors, STD experiences, and needed health engagement with the public. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms Reddit's feasibility and utility for understanding sexual-health topics, matching the preprint's contribution claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Hematologic biomarkers and clinical features can accurately classify unipolar and bipolar depression using machine learning methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning methods can accurately classify unipolar and bipolar depression using clinical features and biomarkers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the central result that ML using clinical features and biomarkers can accurately distinguish unipolar and bipolar depression.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The optimal performance for classification was achieved by XGBoost with an AUC of 0.889, sensitivity of 0.831, specificity of 0.839, and accuracy of 0.863. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: XGBoost achieved an optimal open-test performance with an AUC of 0.889, sensitivity of 0.831, specificity of 0.839, and accuracy of 0.863. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numerical performance metrics and method are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Specific blood biomarkers such as WBC and MONO are informative across different disease durations, while others like NEUT and albumin vary in their indicative value based on disease duration. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The most informative features for the differential diagnosis include clinical features of disease duration and age of onset, biochemical markers, and complete blood count-derived biomarkers. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the preprint's biomarker observations within a broader feature set (clinical duration/age, biochemical and CBC markers), clarifying which categories were most informative.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Longitudinal changes in biomarkers should be investigated across different disease courses and age groups to improve diagnostic accuracy. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The recommendation to investigate longitudinal biomarker changes is not represented among the publication's listed conclusions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The best-performing model estimated gestational age (GA) within 9.5 days overall of reference GA with a mean absolute error (MAE) of 1.35 days. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best-performing model estimated gestational age within 9.5 days overall of reference gestational age in data from heel prick samples. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the sample-type context (heel prick) while retaining the same overall accuracy metrics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In preterm infants and those small for GA, the mean absolute error was 2.62 weeks and 1.81 weeks, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: In preterm infants and those small for gestational age, the mean absolute error was 2.62 weeks and 1.81 weeks, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The reported MAE values for preterm and SGA infants are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Model accuracy slightly decreased in data from cord blood, with a mean absolute error of 1.44 days. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Model accuracy slightly decreased in data from cord blood, with a mean absolute error of 1.44 days. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both versions report the same diminished accuracy for cord blood with identical MAE.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Model performance was diminished in the Kenya cohort compared to internal validation performance using Ontario data. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Accuracy of metabolic model based gestational age estimates in the Kenya cohort was lower compared to previously published validation studies. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains that accuracy was lower for the Kenya cohort but changes the comparator from internal Ontario validation to previously published validation studies, revising the framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The timing of dating ultrasound is critical to develop algorithms for accurate estimation of GA based on metabolic analysis of blood obtained at birth. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Inconsistency in the timing of reference dating ultrasounds appears to have contributed to diminished model performance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies that inconsistent timing of dating ultrasounds likely contributed to poorer performance, which clarifies the preprint claim but with slightly tempered certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The SASKit study primarily aims to discover novel biomarkers for deterioration of health and (co-)morbidities triggered by pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma or ischemic stroke. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study aims to investigate the link between (co)morbidity and ageing by exploring biomarkers and molecular mechanisms in patients with pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma and ischaemic stroke. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the study aim to emphasize links with ageing and molecular mechanisms rather than solely discovering biomarkers for deterioration, broadening the conceptual scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Predictive biomarkers for interventions addressing senescence may become available if the biomarkers that we find are predominantly related to aging / cellular senescence. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Prognostic and predictive biomarkers for interventions addressing senescence may become available if the biomarkers found are specifically related to ageing and cellular senescence. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication expands the claim to include prognostic as well as predictive biomarkers and uses slightly stronger wording about specificity to ageing/senescence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Diagnostic biomarkers will be explored for their relationship to aging / cellular senescence. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study aims to investigate the link between (co)morbidity and ageing by exploring biomarkers and molecular mechanisms in patients with pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma and ischaemic stroke. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication's general aim to explore biomarkers and mechanisms encompasses the preprint's intent to explore diagnostic biomarkers in relation to aging, clarifying the context though with broader emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our findings will require validation in independent studies, and our dataset shall be useful to validate the findings of other studies. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings of this study will require validation in independent studies and the dataset will be useful to validate the findings of other studies. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same limitation and dataset-use statement from the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Four clusters with distinct patterns of PROMs responses were identified among patients undergoing ACL reconstruction.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Four clusters with distinct patterns of patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) responses were identified among patients undergoing ACL reconstruction.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result with near-identical wording and meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Significant differences in PROMs between clusters were observed, with variance explained ranging from 40-69%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Significant differences in PROMs between clusters were observed, with variance explained ranging from 40 to 69%.
alignments.rationale: The variance range and conclusion are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Cluster membership was not significantly associated with patient age, gender, BMI, or injury-to-examination delay.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cluster membership was not significantly associated with patient age, gender, body mass index (BMI), or injury-to-examination delay.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same null associations with only minor wording changes (BMI spelled out).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Patients undergoing ACL reconstruction represent a spectrum of knee function, general physical and mental health, and preinjury activity levels.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients undergoing ACL reconstruction represent a spectrum of knee function, general physical and mental health, and preinjury activity levels.
alignments.rationale: The conceptual conclusion is the same in both versions with no substantive change.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The factors driving distinct responses to PROMs among ACL reconstruction patients remain unknown and are unrelated to common demographic variables.
alignments.rationale: This conclusion appears only in the publication and extends the discussion by explicitly stating that drivers of distinct PROM responses remain unknown and are not demographic.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The iBEAT study aims to determine whether renal imaging biomarkers provide insight into the pathogenesis and heterogeneity of diabetic kidney disease (DKD).
alignments.pub_claim_text: The iBEAt study aims to determine whether renal imaging biomarkers provide insight into the pathogenesis and heterogeneity of diabetic kidney disease (DKD).
alignments.rationale: The aim is stated equivalently in preprint and publication (minor capitalization/spelling differences only).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study will explore the potential associations between imaging biomarkers and renal function.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study will explore the potential associations between imaging biomarkers and renal function.
alignments.rationale: The planned analysis of associations with renal function is described the same way in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The iBEAT study is the largest DKD imaging study to date and aims to contribute to a more personalized approach to the management of DKD in patients with type 2 diabetes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: iBEAt is the largest DKD imaging study to date and will provide valuable insights into the progression and heterogeneity of DKD.
alignments.rationale: Both claim the study is the largest, but the publication shifts emphasis from an explicit personalized-management aim to providing insights into progression/heterogeneity, narrowing the stated downstream impact.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Novel prognostic markers are needed to improve stratification of patients based on risk of disease progression in DKD.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The imaging biomarkers may improve the prediction of DKD progression beyond routinely available data.
alignments.rationale: The preprint's general call for novel prognostic markers is translated in the publication into a specific result/possibility about imaging biomarkers improving prediction, shifting from a general need to a specific potential solution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Retinograd-AI achieves a performance of 91% accuracy on our held-out dataset of 133 images with an Area Under the Receiver Operator Characteristic (AUROC) of 0.94.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Retinograd-AI achieves 91% accuracy on our held-out dataset of 133 images with an area under the receiver operator characteristic curve of 0.94.
alignments.rationale: The numerical performance metrics and dataset size are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The highest proportion of gradable images was found in the 30-50 years age group, where 84.3% of images were rated as gradable.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A small but significant positive association of gradability with age was found (ß = 0.002, P < 0.001).
alignments.rationale: The preprint reports a peak in a specific age bracket while the publication characterizes a continuous positive association with age; the publication provides a clearer, more general statistical description.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Retinograd-AI is the first open-source AI model for automated retinal image quality assessment of FAF images in IRDs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Retinograd-AI is an open-source AI model for automated retinal image quality assessment of FAF images in IRDs.
alignments.rationale: The publication drops the claim of being the "first" open-source model, weakening the novelty assertion while retaining the core fact that it is open-source.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Automated gradability assessment through Retinograd AI enables large scale analysis of retinal images, which is essential for developing good analysis pipelines.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Quality assessment is essential for the deployment of AI algorithms, such as Eye2Gene, into clinical settings.
alignments.rationale: The preprint's point about enabling large-scale analysis is rephrased in the publication as the broader importance of quality assessment for clinical deployment; the core rationale is similar but framed differently.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Due to the diverse nature of IRD pathologies, Retinograd-AI may also be applicable to FAF imaging for other conditions.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not mention or provide evidence for broader applicability to other conditions, so this preprint claim is not retained.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Applying Retinograd-AI to filter images improved the accuracy of a gene prediction classifier from 33.8% to 68.9%.
alignments.rationale: This specific downstream performance improvement appears only in the publication and was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed PAVE model avoids imputation bias by embedding only observed values into vectors.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed Pattern Attention model with Value Embedding (PAVE) avoids imputation bias by embedding only observed values into vectors.
alignments.rationale: The contribution is stated identically in both versions with only minor naming expansion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PAVE outperforms existing models in sepsis onset prediction and mortality prediction tasks.
alignments.pub_claim_text: PAVE outperforms existing models in sepsis onset prediction and mortality prediction tasks.
alignments.rationale: The reported performance superiority is carried through unchanged from preprint to publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The self-attention mechanism in PAVE enhances model interpretability by identifying meaningful medical event patterns related to mortality.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The self-attention mechanism in PAVE enhances model interpretability by identifying meaningful medical event patterns related to mortality.
alignments.rationale: The interpretability claim is presented the same way in both documents.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PAVE improves risk prediction performance by incorporating the values and occurring time of medical events.
alignments.pub_claim_text: PAVE improves risk prediction performance by incorporating the values and occurring time of medical events.
alignments.rationale: The mechanism and claimed benefit are unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed Simple Context U-Net (SCU-Net) method accurately segments breast arterial calcifications in mammograms.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Simple Context U-Net (SCU-Net) method accurately segments breast arterial calcifications in mammograms.
alignments.rationale: The core segmentation accuracy claim is consistent between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The quantification metrics tested show greater than 95% correlation between predicted masks of SCU-Net and ground truth measurements of calcification on breast CT.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The quantification metrics derived from SCU-Net show greater than 95% correlation with ground truth measurements of calcification on breast CT.
alignments.rationale: The high (>95%) correlation between predicted masks and ground truth is preserved in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The calcification volume results yield R²-correlation values ranging from 0.774 to 0.866 for various quantification metrics.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The calcification volume results yield R²-correlation values ranging from 0.798 to 0.843 for various quantification metrics.
alignments.rationale: The publication updates the numeric R² range compared with the preprint; the claim is retained but the exact reported values are revised/clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SCU-Net demonstrates sufficient sensitivity to detect the normal progression of arterial calcifications over time.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Quantification of calcifications based on SCU-Net segmentation has sufficient sensitivity to detect normal progression over time.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about sufficient sensitivity to detect progression is essentially identical between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The combined explainable AI approach identified a paliperidone-indicated subgroup of schizophrenia patients who demonstrate a larger treatment effect than the full randomized sample. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The combined explainable AI approach identified a paliperidone-indicated subgroup of schizophrenia patients who demonstrated a larger treatment effect than the full randomized sample. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Text is effectively identical between preprint and publication, preserving meaning, scope, and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The treatment effect for the identified subgroup had a Cohen’s d of 1.22, significantly higher than the full sample's Cohen’s d of 0.82. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The treatment effect for the identified subgroup had a Cohen’s d of 1.22, significantly higher than the full sample's Cohen’s d of 0.82. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Numeric effect sizes and interpretation are identical in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The explainable rule lists generated from the modeling approach suggest that high disturbance of volition and high uncooperativeness predict membership in the paliperidone-indicated subgroup. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The rule lists generated suggest that high disturbance of volition or high uncooperativeness may predict membership in the paliperidone-indicated subgroup. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication changes the logical operator ('and' -> 'or') and adds hedging ('may'), which alters the rule logic (broadens membership) and lowers certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study validates the use of explainable AI for patient selection in clinical trials, improving interpretability for clinicians. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The modeling approach produced simple logical statements that enhance interpretability for clinicians. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Publication drops the stronger claim about validating patient selection and focuses on interpretability via simple logical statements, narrowing scope and softening confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There were no differences between mTBI and OI groups in brain white matter microstructure from either DTI or NODDI in the seven tracts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There were no significant differences between mTBI and OI groups in brain white matter microstructure from either DTI or NODDI in the seven tracts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Wording is effectively the same (no significant differences); meaning unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Fractional anisotropy and neurite density index in all tracts exhibited a significant positive association with age. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Fractional anisotropy and neurite density index in all tracts exhibited a significant positive association with age. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical association statements in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Mean diffusivity in all tracts exhibited a significant negative association with age in the whole sample. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Mean diffusivity in all tracts exhibited a significant negative association with age in the whole sample. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim is the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: mTBI is associated with relatively minor white matter differences, if any, at the post-acute stage. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: mTBI is associated with relatively minor white matter differences, if any, at the post-acute stage. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion language is preserved between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Longitudinal studies with long-term follow-up are needed to assess brain differences that may evolve at later stages of injury. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Longitudinal studies with long-term follow-up are needed to assess brain differences that may evolve at later stages of injury. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Recommendation for longitudinal follow-up is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: No study was considered eligible for inclusion in the review. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: No study was considered eligible for inclusion in the review. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical statement that no studies met inclusion criteria.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is no evidence to support or refute the use of cell-based therapies in the treatment of patients with COVID-19. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is no evidence to support or refute the use of cell-based therapies for treating patients with COVID-19. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Same conclusion; wording slightly adjusted but meaning and confidence unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A substantial number of ongoing studies are expected to provide valuable evidence regarding cell-based therapies for COVID-19. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A substantial number of ongoing studies should provide valuable evidence to inform researchers and decision-makers in the near future. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Publication rephrases expectation with slightly more specific anticipated impact and timing; meaning is preserved but wording is clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of digital adherence technologies (DATs) for measuring tuberculosis medication adherence is suboptimal and variable. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The limited number of studies available suggests suboptimal and variable performance of DATs for dose monitoring. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication tempers the general conclusion by explicitly citing the limited evidence base, narrowing the basis for the claim and adding caution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: For medication sleeves with phone calls, the reported sensitivity and specificity compared to urine analysis was 70-94% and 0-61%, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: For medication sleeves with phone calls, the reported sensitivity was between 70% and 94%, while the specificity ranged from 0% to 61% compared to urine testing. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Numeric ranges and comparison method are consistent between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: For digital pillboxes, the reported sensitivity and specificity compared to pill count was 25-99% and 69-100%, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication's listed claims do not report the pillbox-specific sensitivity/specificity figures present in the preprint; the result appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The sensitivity of dose detection for ingestible sensors was ≥95% in comparison to directly observed ingestion. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication claims do not repeat the ingestible-sensor sensitivity statistic; this specific result appears to have been omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Participant selection was the most frequent potential source of bias across the articles reviewed. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Participant selection was identified as the most frequent potential source of bias in the studies reviewed. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The core finding about participant selection as the main bias source is preserved; publication presents it more definitively (higher certainty).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MicroRNA profiles were found to predict Recurrence Free Survival (RFS) in osteosarcoma patients. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study reveals the existence of molecularly defined phenotypes associated with outcome independent of clinicopathologic features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication frames the result more broadly as molecular phenotypes associated with outcome; the specific RFS prediction claim is generalized into a wider phenotype–outcome finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The microRNA profiles were independently prognostic of RFS when controlled for metastatic disease at diagnosis and pathologic necrosis following chemotherapy. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study reveals the existence of molecularly defined phenotypes associated with outcome independent of clinicopathologic features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements convey that molecular markers (microRNA) are prognostic of outcome independent of clinicopathologic factors; publication expresses this at the phenotype level.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DNA methylation patterns held prognostic significance in osteosarcoma. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication claims do not explicitly mention DNA methylation prognosticity; this specific molecular result appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results support the existence of molecularly defined phenotypes in osteosarcoma associated with distinct outcomes independent of clinicopathologic features. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study reveals the existence of molecularly defined phenotypes associated with outcome independent of clinicopathologic features. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Preprint conclusion and publication claim are essentially the same regarding molecular phenotypes linked to outcome independent of clinicopathologic features.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These networks may define previously unrecognized osteosarcoma subtypes with distinct molecular context and clinical course. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The previously unrecognized osteosarcoma subtypes have distinct clinical courses and responses to therapy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication makes the tentative preprint suggestion more definite and extends it to responses to therapy, increasing confidence and clarifying clinical implications.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GPT-4 displayed a greater proportion of comprehensive knowledge for the categories of 'basic knowledge' and 'management' at 89.8% and 82.9%, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GPT-4 displayed a greater proportion of comprehensive knowledge for the categories of 'basic knowledge' and 'management' (89.8% and 82.9%, respectively). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same percentages and categories verbatim, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GPT-3.5 performed best in the 'management' and 'other' category with scores of 78.1% and 94.1%, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GPT-3.5 performed best in the 'management' category and 'other' category with comprehensive knowledge scores of 78.1% and 94.1%, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same performance figures and categories for GPT-3.5 with equivalent meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The models provided highly reproducible responses, with GPT-3.5 scoring above 94% in every category and GPT-4 with 100% for all answers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GPT-4 provided 107/107 (100%) responses with correct information. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the GPT-4 100% result but omits the statement about GPT-3.5's >94% reproducibility, narrowing the original combined claim and tempering the overall reproducibility claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ChatGPT may lead to better outcomes in patients with heart failure by providing accessible and easy to understand health education. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: If validated in future studies, ChatGPT may serve as a useful tool in providing accessible health-related information and education to patients living with heart failure. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the benefit more cautiously (tool for information/education if validated) rather than asserting potential for improved outcomes, clarifying and tempering the original causal suggestion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model necessitates further rigorous testing and validation to ensure patient safety and equity across all patient demographics. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ChatGPT necessitates further rigorous testing and validation to ensure patient safety and equity across all patient demographics. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The limitation is restated verbatim, showing no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Cerebral Blood Flow Velocity waveforms can provide evidence for cerebrovascular occlusion and stenosis. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Cerebral Blood Flow Velocity waveforms can provide evidence for cerebrovascular occlusion and stenosis. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An unsupervised machine learning approach can objectively assess TCD morphology for waveform categorization. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: An unsupervised machine learning approach can objectively assess TCD morphology for waveform categorization. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The methodological conclusion is reproduced exactly in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Gap-statistic disparity was maximized at four clusters, indicating distinct flow types associated with control subjects and LVO patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Gap statistic disparity was maximized at four clusters, which were identified as flow types I, II, III, and IV. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds explicit labeling of the four clusters (I–IV) but retains the central result that four clusters are optimal, so the claim is clarified with more detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Types II and III represent commonly observed flow-types that deviate quantifiably from normal and blunted flows, but are not delineated by TIBI. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Types I and II were primarily composed of control subject waveforms, while types III and IV derived mainly from LVO patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication recharacterizes the composition of cluster types (I/II vs III/IV) rather than stating that II and III deviate and are not delineated by TIBI, representing a substantive revision of the specific type interpretations.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Important morphological variability exists beyond that currently quantified by TIBI in populations at-risk for acute ischemic stroke. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Important morphological variability exists beyond that currently quantified by TIBI in populations experiencing or at-risk for acute ischemic stroke. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication broadens the scope slightly by including both 'experiencing or at-risk' populations and clarifies the target population, but the core conclusion remains.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A 14-gene expression signature can successfully predict benefit to the proteasome inhibitor bortezomib in Multiple Myeloma patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: STLsig identified two gene networks that can predict benefit to the proteasome inhibitor, bortezomib, in a dataset of 910 patients with multiple myeloma. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports predictive gene structures (two gene networks) and a large dataset; this appears to be a recharacterization of the originally reported signature (14 genes) rather than a direct verbatim match.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The identified gene expression signature demonstrates robustness, as it shows similar classification performance in an independent patient cohort measured on a different platform. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not state an independent-cohort/different-platform validation as described in the preprint, so this claim appears to be omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The genes in the discovered signature are essential, as no equivalent signature can be found when they are excluded from the analysis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: No equivalent signature can be found when the genes in the signature are excluded from the analysis, indicating that they are essential. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same finding about the essential nature of the signature's genes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Multiple genes in the signature are linked to working mechanisms of proteasome inhibitors or Multiple Myeloma disease progression. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: STLsig can identify gene signatures that could aid in treatment decisions for patients with multiple myeloma and provide insight into the biological mechanism behind treatment benefit. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the mechanistic links as providing insight into treatment benefit; this clarifies and contextualizes the original mechanistic claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method introduced allows for identification of gene expression signatures that can aid in treatment decisions for Multiple Myeloma patients. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: STLsig can identify gene signatures that could aid in treatment decisions for patients with multiple myeloma and provide insight into the biological mechanism behind treatment benefit. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the method's utility for treatment decisions but softens certainty (medium) and adds emphasis on mechanistic insight, clarifying and slightly tempering the original high-certainty claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Heart rate (ECG), vasomotor activity (PPG), and pulse arrival time (ECG+PPG) provided sufficient information to determine target engagement in addition to other combinations of sensors. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Heart rate (ECG), vasomotor activity (PPG), and pulse arrival time (ECG+PPG) provided sufficient information to determine target engagement in addition to other combinations of sensors. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this sensor-finding verbatim, indicating no change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study achieved 96% accuracy, precision, and recall with a receiver operator characteristics area of 0.96 in detecting active tcVNS stimulation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study achieved 96% accuracy, precision, and recall with a receiver operator characteristics area of 0.96 in detecting active tcVNS stimulation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Performance metrics are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Two commonly utilized sensing modalities (ECG and PPG) that are suitable for home use can provide useful information on therapy response for tcVNS. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Two commonly utilized sensing modalities (ECG and PPG) that are suitable for home use can provide useful information on therapy response for tcVNS. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The home-use sensing conclusion is carried forward without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The methods presented could be deployed in wearable devices to quantify adherence for at-home use of tcVNS technologies. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The methods presented could be deployed in wearable devices to quantify adherence for at-home use of tcVNS technologies. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The deployment/translation claim remains the same in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep neural networks trained to predict morbidity risk from wearable sensor data can provide a high-quality and cheap alternative for biological age acceleration (BAA) determination. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep neural networks trained to predict morbidity risk from wearable sensor data can provide a high-quality and cheap alternative for biological age acceleration (BAA) determination. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the core claim about neural-network-derived BAA from wearable data unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The GeroSense BAA model exhibited a superior association with life-expectancy over the average number of steps per day. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The GeroSense BAA model exhibited a superior association with life-expectancy over the average number of steps per day in groups stratified by professional occupations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies that the superior association was observed in groups stratified by occupation, narrowing the originally broader statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The association between BAA and effects of lifestyles, the prevalence or future incidence of diseases was comparable to that of BAA from models based on blood test results. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The association between BAA and effects of lifestyles, as well as the prevalence of future incidence of diseases, was comparable to that of BAA from models based on blood test results. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the association claim with equivalent meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The number of individuals suffering from the lack of resilience increased exponentially with age at a rate compatible with Gompertz mortality law. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The number of individuals suffering from the lack of resilience increased exponentially with age at a rate compatible with Gompertz mortality law. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The quantitative resilience–age relationship is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The mean and auto-correlation properties of BAA fluctuations comprise the minimum set of biomarkers of aging in humans. (certainty=low, type=speculation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The mean and auto-correlation properties of BAA fluctuations together comprise the minimum set of biomarkers of aging in humans. (certainty=low, type=speculation)
alignments.rationale: The speculative biomarker claim is essentially identical between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Treatment that directly influences the neural circuit relevant to an individual patient’s subset of symptoms might more precisely and effectively aid in the alleviation of their specific symptoms.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A novel treatment using fMRI functional connectivity neurofeedback can effectively aid in alleviating specific depressive symptoms by normalizing neural connectivity.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the preprint idea with a specific method (fMRI FC neurofeedback) and stronger affirmative language, clarifying the mechanism and increasing stated confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The targeted biomarker was the functional connectivity between the left dorsolateral prefrontal cortex and left precuneus, which is less anticorrelated in patients with melancholic depression than in healthy controls.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Patients with melancholic depression exhibit less anticorrelated connectivity between the left dorsolateral prefrontal cortex/middle frontal gyrus and the left precuneus/posterior cingulate cortex compared to healthy controls.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same core finding but expands anatomical labels (adds MFG/PCC), clarifying and slightly broadening the loci while maintaining similar confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The more a participant normalized this biomarker, the more related depressive symptoms were reduced.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Normalization of the targeted neural connectivity is correlated with a reduction in depressive and brooding symptoms, but not in unrelated symptoms like trait anxiety.
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies which symptoms tracked the biomarker and explicitly excludes unrelated symptoms, narrowing the scope and reporting a more cautious confidence level.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: One-to-one correspondence between a normalized neural network and decreased depressive symptoms was demonstrated.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Normalization of the targeted neural connectivity is correlated with a reduction in depressive and brooding symptoms, but not in unrelated symptoms like trait anxiety.
alignments.rationale: The preprint's strong 'one-to-one correspondence' claim is weakened in the publication to a correlation limited to specific symptom types, reducing scope and strength.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The effects of the treatment remained one to two months later, indicating potential clinical utility.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The effects of the neurofeedback treatment remained observable one to two months after the intervention.
alignments.rationale: Both versions report the same persistence of effects at one to two months with similar certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed neural network extension to the SEIRD model can predict the infection rate as a function of time and disease prevalence.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed neural network model can predict the infection rate as a function of both time and the disease’s prevalence.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same modeling capability with equivalent scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model is capable of exhibiting both single peak and multi-peak behavior, which reproduces observed behaviors in various counties.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model can exhibit both single peak and multi-peak behavior, reproducing behavior observed in counties both in and out of the training set.
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies that the model reproduces behavior including out-of-training counties, broadening the claimed generalizability while keeping confidence high.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed extensions to the SEIRD model are necessary to explain the spread of COVID-19, as demonstrated by a numerical comparison of simulation errors.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed model substantially reduces errors compared to a standard SEIRD model.
alignments.rationale: The preprint's necessity claim is toned down in the publication to an empirical improvement (error reduction) versus a standard model, changing the nature of the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Resistance to population-level host immunity has increasingly shaped SARS-CoV-2 evolution over time.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We found evidence that ACE2-mediated transmissibility and resistance to population-level host immunity has waxed and waned as a primary driver of SARS-CoV-2 evolution over time.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents a more nuanced, non-monotonic ('waxed and waned') role for immunity resistance rather than an increasing trend, and reports this with somewhat lower stated certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Mutations that will spread can be identified with high accuracy up to four months in advance across different phases of the pandemic.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We retroactively identified with high accuracy mutations that will spread, at up to four months in advance, across different phases of the pandemic.
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the same claim of high-accuracy identification up to four months across pandemic phases; wording ('retroactively identified') matches evaluation methodology but preserves the core claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model forecasts mutations that will spread in the future and characterizes how these mutations affect the binding of therapeutic antibodies.
alignments.pub_claim_text: These findings demonstrate that it is possible to forecast the driver mutations that could appear in emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants of concern.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes forecasting driver mutations but omits explicit claims that the model characterizes effects on antibody binding, thus narrowing and somewhat tempering the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This modeling approach may be applied to any pathogen with genomic surveillance data, potentially addressing other rapidly evolving pathogens.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This modeling approach may be applied to any rapidly evolving pathogens with sufficiently dense genomic surveillance data.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds qualifiers (rapidly evolving pathogens, sufficiently dense data), clarifying the applicability and narrowing the claim's scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed model yielded an Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristics (AUROC) of 0.920.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed model yielded an area under the receiver operating characteristics (AUROC) of 0.930, indicating high predictive accuracy for unplanned 30-day readmission of ICU patients with heart failure.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a slightly different AUROC value (0.930 vs 0.920), so the numeric result is revised though the overall performance claim remains high.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed approach was capable of modeling the time-related variables and incorporating the medical history of patients from prior hospital visits for prediction.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model effectively incorporated time-related variables and the medical history of patients from prior hospital visits for prediction.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the capability as effective but presents it with slightly lower stated certainty, clarifying performance while tempering confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our approach significantly improved the outcome prediction compared to that of other ML-based models and health calculators.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model demonstrated a precision of 0.886, sensitivity of 0.805, accuracy of 0.841, and F-score of 0.800, which were superior to the best baseline model and existing literature.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides detailed metrics supporting the preprint's claim of significant improvement, preserving the claim and its confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Stimulating SMA had an inhibitory influence on M1 excitability OFF levodopa.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Stimulating the supplementary motor area (SMA) inhibits primary motor cortex (M1) excitability OFF levodopa and facilitates M1 excitability ON levodopa.
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly contains the same OFF-levodopa inhibitory effect as the preprint with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Stimulating SMA had a facilitatory influence on M1 excitability ON levodopa.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Stimulating the supplementary motor area (SMA) inhibits primary motor cortex (M1) excitability OFF levodopa and facilitates M1 excitability ON levodopa.
alignments.rationale: The publication states the same ON-levodopa facilitation effect as the preprint; it is part of the combined Q0 claim.
